title: `(apply loop lst)` is never tail-call-optimized — call_apply_codegen never consults TailCallContext
goal: call_apply_codegen.cpp compiles every `apply` call to a genuine (non-tail) LLVM `call`, regardless of whether the target is the enclosing function's own self-recursive loop. Using `(apply loop next-args)` as a long-running loop's back-edge therefore grows the native call stack by one frame per iteration no matter what the target function's body looks like, unlike a direct `(loop arg1 arg2 ...)` call which does participate in TCO. Documented as a known, unfixed gap in docs/LONG_RUNNING_LOOPS.md (Q3) and noted as an explicit non-goal inside ESH-0222's fix (guard-tail-position), but never had its own tracked ticket. Filed here as a clean follow-up: make `apply` consult `isTCOActive`/`TailCallContext` the same way ordinary self-calls do when the applied function is provably the active TCO target, so `(apply loop lst)` becomes a legitimate flat loop back-edge.
next_actions: Read call_apply_codegen.cpp's apply-call lowering and compare with the ordinary self-call TCO path in tail_call_codegen.cpp to find where the tail-position check needs to hook in.

Determine statically when `apply`'s target is provably the active TCO loop function (e.g. `(apply loop args)` where `loop` names the enclosing named-let/define being compiled) vs a dynamically-unknown target, and gate the optimization to the provable case only.
Add a regression test exercising `(apply loop (list ...))` for >= 10,000,000 iterations under a reduced stack ulimit, mirroring tests/tco/named_let_tail_positions_test.esk.
Update docs/LONG_RUNNING_LOOPS.md Q3 once fixed; until then it accurately documents the gap and should not be edited.
acceptance: `(define (f) (let loop ((n 0)) (if (>= n N) n (apply loop (list (+ n 1)))))) ` runs flat (O(1) stack) to at least N=10,000,000 when the apply target is the loop's own self-recursive call.
Dynamically-dispatched `apply` calls (target not statically the active loop) remain correctly non-tail — no regression to general `apply` semantics.
scripts/run_stress.sh and the existing tco test suite stay green.
ux_payoff: Closes the last documented gap in Eshkol's two idiomatic tail-loop forms (named-let and apply-based dispatch); today users must be steered away from a natural-looking pattern with no compiler diagnostic.
